metadata.file_name: 60. Bozkuş. (2025). Predictors of reading performance of fourth-grade Turkish students.pdf
metadata.title: Predictors of reading performance of fourth-grade Turkish students

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PIRLS 2021 microdata for fourth-grade students in Türkiye, merged from student, teacher, home, and school questionnaires into a single R-processed file. The analytic sample was restricted to cases with <=20% missing data, after which remaining missing values were imputed and the dataset was normalized (centering and variance scaling). The manuscript does not report applying PIRLS sampling weights or any replicate-weight procedure (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and it does not name any weight variable (such as W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT). Because the predictive model is an SVM trained with caret and the paper does not discuss complex-survey weighting, the analysis is treated as unweighted; this may leave residual bias from the original clustered sampling design.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PIRLS 2021 fourth-grade data for Türkiye. The consolidated raw file contained 6032 students; cases with more than 20% missing data were removed, reducing the sample to 5538. Remaining missing values were imputed using k-nearest-neighbor mean imputation with k=10. The dependent variable was constructed by recoding the PIRLS benchmark score ASRIBM01 (first plausible value) into a binary success indicator: low performance combined categories below 400 and 400–<475; high performance combined categories 475–<550? (as described: categories 4 and 5: 550–<625 and >=625). The final analytic dataset for ML contained 3589 students and 406 variables (405 predictors plus the binary outcome identifier).

main_findings.target_variable: Reading performance (binary high vs low; ASRIBM01 PV1 benchmark)
main_findings.performance_metrics: SVM (linear kernel, L2-regularized) — Accuracy: 0.957 (95% CI: 0.943–0.968), Kappa: 0.914, Sensitivity: 0.956, Specificity: 0.958, Balanced Accuracy: 0.9572; P-value [Acc > NIR] < 0.001. Feature selection via RFE identified top 16 factors; model trained/tested with 70%/30% split and 5-times repeated 10-fold CV for resampling.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PIRLS 2021 Türkiye (Grade 4) Reading the study leveraged school emphasis on foundational literacy (letter-sound and alphabet letters, reading words) together with access to books (borrowing from classroom library) and teacher assessment practices to predict binary reading performance, finding that the SVM distinguished high- vs low-performing students with high accuracy (≈95.7%) and strong agreement (Kappa ≈0.914). The top predictors were dominated by school-level instructional emphasis and book access, followed by teacher assessment/teaching strategies and motivation-related factors, while only one family-level factor (parental commitment to readiness) entered the top 16. This indicates that ol-level instructional emphasis and book access, followed by teacher assessment/teaching strategies and motivation-related factors, while only one family-level factor (parental commitment to readiness) entered the top 16.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzed PIRLS 2021 Grade 4 reading data for Türkiye (final analytic sample: 3589 students) and recoded the benchmark score ASRIBM01 (first plausible value) into a binary outcome (high vs low performance). Using an L2-regularized linear-kernel SVM, trained with recursive feature elimination (RFE) over 405 candidate predictors and evaluated with 5-times repeated 10-fold cross-validation plus a 70/30 train-test split, the model achieved Accuracy = 0.957 (95% CI: 0.943–0.968), Kappa = 0.914, Sensitivity = 0.956, Specificity = 0.958, and Balanced Accuracy = 0.9572 (P < 0.001 vs no-information rate). The most influential predictors were school-level emphases on foundational literacy (letter-sound relationships, alphabet letters, reading words) and students’ ability to borrow books, with teacher-level assessment and comprehension-support strategies also prominent. A key limitation is that the paper does not report using PIRLS sampling weights/replicate weights, and the outcome is a binary classification derived from PV1 benchmark recoding rather than modeling the full achievement scale.
